description: A person or group who shares the keeping of a dwelling and its daily arrangements over time. A person living alone can be a household. Relatives in different homes need not be one. 'womu' (home) is the place of dwelling and belonging, while 'womuri' identifies the domestic unit that keeps life there.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move into true-mid rounded 'o'. Close the lips for 'm', carry voicing through the nose, and hold stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before final high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Rounded 'wo' enters the stressed lip closure and deep nasal vowel of 'mu'. The rounded opening is 'womu' (home) heard intact. Final 'ri' releases the mouth with a quick tongue tap and bright 'i'.
usage_notes: Each household may be counted, and a preceding dwelling or member can mark possession. 'lumani' names a family relation, while 'sila' names a community whose life need not centre on one dwelling. Governance vocabulary becomes relevant when several households share resources or make decisions together.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A household shares heat, tools, food, and care at the scale where daily use is easiest to see and change.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The word lets speakers describe who shares domestic life while kinship, ownership, and harmony wait for their own claims.

semantic_domains.community: a person or group sharing domestic arrangements
semantic_domains.spatial: daily life organized around a dwelling
